title: Regret Bounds and Reinforcement Learning Exploration of EXP-based Algorithms

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## REGRET BOUNDS AND REINFORCEMENT LEARNING EXPLORATION OF EXP-BASED ALGORITHMS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

EXP-based algorithms are often used for exploration in multi-armed bandit. We revisit the EXP3.P algorithm and establish both the lower and upper bounds of regret in the Gaussian multi-armed bandit setting, as well as a more general distribution option. The analyses do not require bounded rewards compared to classical regret assumptions. We also extend EXP4 from multi-armed bandit to reinforcement learning to incentivize exploration by multiple agents. The resulting algorithm has been tested on hard-to-explore games and it shows an improvement on exploration compared to state-of-the-art.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Multi-armed bandit (MAB) is to maximize cumulative reward of a player throughout a bandit game by choosing different arms at each time step. It is also equivalent to minimizing the regret defined as the difference between the best rewards that can be achieved and the actual reward gained by the player. Formally, given time horizon _T_ , in time step _t ≤ T_ the player choose one arm _at_ among _K_ arms, receives _ra[t] t_[among rewards] _[ r][t]_[= (] _[r]_ 1 _[t][, r]_ 2 _[t][, . . . , r] K[t]_[)][, and maximizes the total reward][ �] _[T] t_ =1 _[r] a[t] t_ or minimizes the regret. Computationally efficient and with abundant theoretical analyses are the EXP-type MAB algorithms. In EXP3.P, each arm has a trust coefficient (weight). The player samples each arm with probability being the sum of its normalized weights and a bias term, receives reward of the sampled arm and exponentially updates the weights based on the corresponding reward estimates. It achieves the regret of the order _O_ ( _√T_ ) in a high probability sense. In EXP4, there are any number of experts. Each has a sample rule over actions and a weight. The player samples according to the weighted average of experts’ sample rules and updates the weights respectively.

Contextual bandit is a variant of MAB by adding context or state space _S_ . At time step _t_ , the player has context _st ∈ S_ with _s_ 1: _T_ = ( _s_ 1 _, s_ 2 _, . . . , sT_ ) being independent. Rewards _r[t]_ follow _F_ ( _µ_ ( _st_ )) where _F_ is any distribution and _µ_ ( _st_ ) is the mean vector that depends on state _st_ . Reinforcement Learning (RL) generalizes contextual bandit, where state and reward transitions follow a Markov Decision Process (MDP) represented by transition kernel _P_ ( _st_ +1 _, r[t] |at, st_ ). A key challenge in RL is the trade-off between exploration and exploitation. Exploration is to encourage the player to try new arms in MAB or new actions in RL to understand the game better. It helps to plan for the future, but with the sacrifice of potentially lowering the current reward. Exploitation aims to exploit currently known states and arms to maximize the current reward, but it potentially prevents the player to gain more information to increase local reward. To maximize the cumulative reward, the player needs to know the game by exploration, while guaranteeing current reward by exploitation.

How to incentivize exploration in RL has been a main focus in RL. Since RL is built on MAB, it is natural to extend MAB techniques to RL and UCB is such a success. UCB (Auer et al. (2002a)) motivates count-based exploration (Strehl and Littman, 2008) in RL and the subsequent PseudoCount exploration (Bellemare et al., 2016). New deep RL exploration algorithms have been recently proposed. Using deep neural networks to keep track of the _Q_ -values by means of _Q_ -networks in RL is called DQN (Mnih et al. (2013)). This combination of deep learning and RL has shown great success. _ϵ_ -greedy in Mnih et al. (2015) is a simple exploration technique using DQN. Besides _ϵ_ -greedy, intrinsic model exploration computes intrinsic rewards by focusing on experiences. Intrinsic rewards directly measure and incentivize exploration if added to extrinsic (actual) rewards of RL, e.g. DORA (Fox et al., 2018) and (Stadie et al., 2015). Random Network Distillation (RND) (Burda et al., 2018) is a more recent suggestion relying on a fixed target network. A drawback of RND is its local focus without global exploration.

In order to address weak points of these various exploration algorithms in the RL context, the notion of experts is natural and thus EXP-type MAB algorithms are appropriate. The allowance of arbitrary experts provides exploration for harder contextual bandits and hence providing exploration possibilities for RL. We develop an EXP4 exploration algorithm for RL that relies on several general experts. This is the first RL algorithm using several exploration experts enabling global exploration. Focusing on DQN, in the computational study we focus on two agents consisting of RND and _ϵ_ -greedy DQN.

We implement the RL EXP4 algorithm on the hard-to-explore RL game Montezuma’s Revenge and compare it with the benchmark algorithm RND (Burda et al. (2018)). The numerical results show that the algorithm gains more exploration than RND and it gains the ability of global exploration by not getting stuck in local maximums of RND. Its total reward also increases with training. Overall, our algorithm improves exploration and exploitation on the benchmark game and demonstrates a learning process in RL.

Reward in RL in many cases is unbounded which relates to unbounded MAB rewards. There are three major versions of MAB: Adversarial, Stochastic, and herein introduced Gaussian. For adversarial MAB, rewards of the _K_ arms _r[t]_ can be chosen arbitrarily by adversaries at step _t_ . For stochastic MAB, the rewards at different steps are assumed to be i.i.d. and the rewards across arms are independent. It is assumed that 0 _≤ ri[t][≤]_[1][for][any][arm] _[i]_[and][step] _[t]_[.][For][Gaussian][MAB,][rewards] _[r][t]_[follow] multi-variate normal _N_ ( _µ,_ Σ) with _µ_ being the mean vector and Σ the covariance matrix of the _K_ arms. Here the rewards are neither bounded, nor independent among the arms. For this reason the introduced Gaussian MAB reflects the RL setting and is the subject of our MAB analyses of EXP3.P. EXP-type algorithms (Auer et al. (2002b)) are optimal in the two classical MABs. Auer et al. (2002b) show lower and upper bounds on regret of the order _O_ ( _√T_ ) for adversarial MAB and of the order _O_ (log( _T_ )) for stochastic MAB. All of the proofs of these regret bounds by EXP-type algorithms are based on the bounded reward assumption, which does not hold for Gaussian MAB. Therefore, the regret bounds for Gaussian MAB with unbounded rewards studied herein are significantly different from prior works. We show both lower and upper bounds on regret of Gaussian MAB under certain assumptions. Some analyses even hold for more generally distributed MAB. Upper bounds borrow some ideas from the analysis of the EXP3.P algorithm in Auer et al. (2002b) for bounded MAB to our unbounded MAB, while lower bounds are by our brand new construction of instances. Precisely, we derive lower bounds of order Ω( _T_ ) for certain fixed _T_ and upper bounds of order _O[∗]_ ( _√T_ ) for _T_ being large enough. The question of bounds for any value of _T_ remains open.

The main contributions of this work are as follows. On the analytical side we introduce Gaussian MAB with the unique aspect and challenge of unbounded rewards. We provide the very first regret lower bound in such a case by constructing a novel family of Gaussian bandits and we are able to analyze the EXP3.P algorithm for Gaussian MAB. Unbounded reward poses a non-trivial challenge in the analyses. We also provide the very first extension of EXP4 to RL exploration. We show its superior performance on two hard-to-explore RL games.

A literature review is provided in Section 2. Then in Section 3 we exhibit upper bounds for unbounded MAB of the EXP3.P algorithm and lower bounds, respectively. Section 4 discusses the EXP4 algorithm for RL exploration. Finally, in Section 5, we present numerical results related to the proposed algorithm.

## 2 LITERATURE REVIEW

The importance of exploration in RL is well understood. Count-based exploration in RL relies on UCB. Strehl and Littman (2008) develop Bellman value iteration _V_ ( _s_ ) = max _a R_[ˆ] ( _s, a_ ) + _γE_ [ _V_ ( _s[′]_ )] + _βN_ ( _s, a_ ) _[−]_[1] 2 , where _N_ ( _s, a_ ) is the number of visits to ( _s, a_ ) for state _s_ and action _a_ . Value _N_ ( _s, a_ ) _[−]_[1] 2 is positively correlated with curiosity of ( _s, a_ ) and encourages exploration. This method is limited to tableau model-based MDP for small state spaces, while Bellemare et al. (2016) introduce Pseudo-Count exploration for non-tableau MDP with density models.

In conjunction with DQN, _ϵ_ -greedy in Mnih et al. (2015) is a simple exploration technique using DQN. Besides _ϵ_ -greedy, intrinsic model exploration computes intrinsic rewards by the accuracy of a model trained on experiences. Intrinsic rewards directly measure and incentivize exploration if

Intrinsic reward _e_ ( _s, a_ ) relies on stochastic transition from _s_ to _s[′]_ and brings noise to exploration. Random Network Distillation(RND) in Burda et al. (2018) addresses this by defining _e_ ( _s, a_ ) = _||f_[ˆ] ( _s[′]_ ) _− f_ ( _s[′]_ ) _||_ 2[2][where] _[f]_[ˆ][is a parametric] model and _f_ is a randomly initialized but fixed model. Here _e_ ( _s, a_ ), independent of the transition, only depends on state _s[′]_ and drives RND to outperform other algorithms on Montezuma’s Revenge. None of these algorithms use several experts which is a significant departure from our work.

In terms of MAB regret analyses focusing on EXP-type algorithms, Auer et al. (2002b) first introduce EXP3.P for bounded adversarial MAB and EXP4 for contextual bandits. Under the EXP3.P algorithm, an upper bound on regret of the order _O_ ( _√T_ ) is achieved, which has no gap with the lower bound and hence it establishes that EXP3.P is optimal. However these regret bounds are not applicable to Gaussian MAB since rewards can be infinite. Meanwhile for unbounded MAB, Srinivas et al. (2010) demonstrate a regret bound of order _O_ ( _[√] T · γT_ ) for noisy Gaussian process bandits where a reward observation contains noise. The information gain _γT_ is not well-defined in a noiseless Gaussian setting. For noiseless Gaussian bandits, Grünewälder et al. (2010) show both the optimal lower and upper bounds on regret, but the regret definition is not consistent with the one used in Auer et al. (2002b). We establish a lower bound of the order Ω( _T_ ) for certain _T_ and an upper bound of the order _O[∗]_ ( _√T_ ) asymptotically on regret of unbounded noiseless Gaussian MAB following standard definitions of regret.

## 3.1 LOWER BOUNDS ON REGRET

In this section we derive a lower bound for Gaussian and general MAB under an assumption. General MAB replaces Gaussian with a general distribution. The main technique is to construct instances or sub-classes that have certain regret, no matter what strategies are deployed. We need the following assumption or setting.

**Assumption 1** There are two types of arms with general _K_ with one type being superior ( _S_ is the set of superior arms) and the other being inferior ( _I_ is the set of inferior arms). Let 1 _− q, q_ be the proportions of the superior and inferior arms, respectively which is known to the adversary and clearly 0 _≤ q ≤_ 1. The arms in _S_ are indistinguishable and so are those in _I_ . The first pull of the player has two steps. In the first step the player selects an inferior or superior set of arms based on _P_ ( _S_ ) = 1 _− q_ and _P_ ( _I_ ) = _q_ and once a set is selected, the corresponding reward of an arm from the selected set is received.
An interesting special case of Assumption 1 is the case of two arms and _q_ = 1 _/_ 2. In this case, the player has no prior knowledge and in the first pull chooses an arm uniformly at random.

## 4 EXP4 ALGORITHM FOR RL

EXP4 has shown great success in contextual bandits. Therefore, in this section, we extend EXP4 to RL and develop EXP4-RL illustrated in Algorithm 2.

The player has experts that are represented by deep _Q_ -networks trained by RL algorithms (there is a one to one correspondence between the experts and _Q_ -networks). Each expert also has a trust coefficient. Trust coefficients are also updated exponentially based on the reward estimates as in EXP4. At each step of one episode, the player samples an expert ( _Q_ -network) with probability that is proportional to the weighted average of expert’s trust coefficients. Then _ϵ_ -greedy DQN is applied on the chosen _Q_ -network. Here different from EXP4, the player needs to store all the interaction tuples

The basic idea is the same as EXP4 by using the experts that give advice vectors with deep _Q_ -networks. It is a combination of deep neural networks with EXP4 updates. From a different perspective, we can also view it as an ensemble in classification (Xia et al. (2011)), by treating _Q_ -networks as ensembles in RL, instead of classification algorithms. While _Q_ -networks do not necessarily have to be experts, i.e., other experts can be used, these are natural in a DQN framework.

In our implementation and experiments we use two experts, thus _E_ = 2 with two _Q_ -networks. The first one is based on RND (Burda et al. (2018)) while the second one is a simple DQN. (2018). This value is then added to the 4-tuple pushed to _B_ . Intuitively, Algorithm 2 circumvents this drawback with the total exploration guided by two experts with EXP4 updated trust coefficients. When the RND expert drives high exploration, its trust coefficient leads to a high total exploration. When it has low exploration, the second expert DQN should have a high one and it incentivizes the total exploration accordingly. Trust coefficients are updated by reward estimates iteratively as in EXP4, so they keep track of the long-term performance of experts and then guide the total exploration globally. These dynamics of EXP4 combined with intrinsic rewards guarantees global exploration. The experimental results exhibited in the next section verify this intuition regarding exploration behind Algorithm 2.

We point out that potentially more general RL algorithms based on _Q_ -factors can be used, e.g., boostrapped DQN (Osband et al. (2016)), random prioritized DQN (Osband et al. (2018)) or adaptive _ϵ_ -greedy VDBE (Tokic (2010)) are a possibility. Furthermore, experts in EXP4 can even be policy networks trained by PPO (Schulman et al. (2017)) instead of DQN for exploration. These possibilities demonstrate the flexibility of the EXP4-RL algorithm.

## 5 COMPUTATIONAL STUDY

As a numerical demonstration of the superior performance and exploration incentive of Algorithm 2, we show the improvements on baselines on two hard-to-explore RL games, Mountain Car and Montezuma’s Revenge. More precisely, we present that the real reward on Mountain Car improves significantly by Algorithm 2 in Section 5.1. Then we implement Algorithm 2 on Montezuma’s Revenge and show the growing and remarkable improvement of exploration in Section 5.2.

(2018) as introduced in Sections 2 and 4. We use the same criterion for evaluating exploration performance of our algorithm and RND herein. RND incentivizes local exploration with the single step intrinsic reward but with the absence of global exploration.

## 5.1 MOUNTAIN CAR

In this part, we summarize the experimental results of Algorithm 2 on Mountain Car, a classical control RL game. This game has very sparse positive rewards, which brings the necessity and hardness of exploration. Blog post (Rivlin (2019)) shows that RND based on DQN improves the performance of traditional DQN, since RND has intrinsic reward to incentivize exploration. We use RND on DQN from Rivlin (2019) as the baseline and show the real reward improvement of Algorithm 2, which supports the intuition and superiority of the algorithm.

The comparison between Algorithm 2 and RND is presented in Figure 1. Here the x-axis is the epoch number and the y-axis is the cumulative reward of that epoch. Figure 1a shows the raw data comparison between EXP4-RL and RND. We observe that though at first RND has several spikes exceeding those of EXP4-RL, EXP4-RL has much higher rewards than RND after 300 epochs. Overall, the relative difference of areas under the curve (AUC) is 4.9% for EXP4-RL over RND, which indicates the significant improvement of our algorithm. This improvement is better illustrated in Figure 1b with the smoothed reward values. Here there is a notable difference between EXP4-RL and RND. Note that the maximum reward hit by EXP4-RL is _−_ 86 and the one by RND is _−_ 118, which additionally demonstrates our improvement on RND.

5.2 MONTEZUMA’S REVENGE AND PURE EXPLORATION SETTING In this section, we show the experimental details of Algorithm 2 on Montezuma’s Revenge, another notoriously hard-to-explore RL game. The benchmark on Montezuma’s Revenge is RND based on DQN which achieves a reward of zero in our environment (the PPO algorithm reported in Burda et al. (2018) has reward 8,000 with many more computing resources; we ran the PPO-based RND with 10 parallel environments and 800 epochs to observe that the reward is also 0), which indicates that DQN has room for improvement regarding exploration.

To this end, we first implement the DQN-version RND (called simply RND hereafter) on Montezuma’s Revenge as our benchmark by replacing the PPO with DQN. Then we implement Algorithm 2 with two experts as aforementioned. Our computing environment allows at most 10 parallel environments. In subsequent figures the x-axis always corresponds to the number of epochs. RND update probability is the proportion of experience that are used for training the intrinsic model _f_[ˆ] (Burda et al., 2018).

A comparison between Algorithm 2 (EXP4-RL) and RND without parallel environments (the update probability is 100% since it is a single environment) is shown in Figure 2 with the emphasis on exploration by means of the intrinsic reward. We use 3 different numbers of burn-in periods (58, 68, 167 burn-in epochs) to remove the initial training steps, which is common in Gibbs sampling. Overall EXP4-RL outperforms RND with many significant spikes in the intrinsic rewards. The larger the number of burn-in periods is, the more significant is the dominance of EXP4-RL over RND. EXP4-RL has much higher exploration than RND at some epochs and stays close to RND at other epochs. At some epochs, EXP4-RL even has 6 times higher exploration. The relative difference in the areas under the curves are 6.9%, 17.0%, 146.0%, respectively, which quantifies the much better performance of EXP4-RL.

Figure 3: The performance of Algorithm 2 and RND with 10 parallel environments and with RND update probability 0.25 and 0.125, measured by loss and intrinsic reward.
We next compare EXP4-RL and RND with 10 parallel environments and different RND update probabilities in Figure 3. The experiences are generated by the 10 parallel environments.

Figure 3a shows that both experts in EXP4-RL are learning with decreasing losses of their _Q_ -networks. The drop is steeper for the RND expert but it starts with a higher loss. With RND update probability 0.25 in Figure 3b we observe that EXP4-RL and RND are very close when RND exhibits high exploration. When RND is at its local minima, EXP4-RL outperforms it. Usually these local minima are driven by sticking to local maxima and then training the model intensively at local maxima, typical of the RND local exploration behavior. EXP4-RL improves on RND as training progresses, e.g. the improvement after 550 epochs is higher than the one between epochs 250 and 550. In terms for AUC, this is expressed by 1.6% and 3.5%, respectively. Overall, EXP4-RL improves RND local minima of exploration, keeps high exploration of RND and induces a smoother global exploration.

With the update probability of 0.125 in Figure 3c, EXP4-RL almost always outperforms RND with a notable difference. The improvement also increases with epochs and is dramatically larger at RND’s local minima. These local minima appear more frequently in training of RND, so our improvement is more significant as well as crucial. The relative AUC improvement is 49.4%. The excellent performance in Figure 3c additionally shows that EXP4-RL improves RND with global exploration by improving local minima of RND or not staying at local maxima.

Overall, with either 0.25 or 0.125, EXP4-RL incentivizes global exploration on RND by not getting stuck in local exploration maxima and outperforms RND exploration aggressively. With 0.125 the improvement with respect to RND is more significant and steady. These experimental evidence verifies our intuition behind EXP4-RL and provides excellent support for it. With experts being more advanced RL exploration algorithms, e.g. DORA, EXP4-RL can bring additional possibilities.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper attempts to extend EXP3.P regret bounds to Gaussian Multi-Armed Bandits (MAB) with unbounded rewards and proposes an EXP4-based algorithm for RL exploration. While the motivation is clear, the theoretical framework lacks the necessary rigor regarding the treatment of unbounded rewards, and the empirical evaluation of the proposed RL algorithm requires more transparency regarding the baseline comparisons.

The paper addresses a challenging problem by attempting to bridge classical MAB theory with unbounded reward distributions and RL. However, the theoretical results, particularly the transition from bounded to unbounded reward analysis, are not sufficiently detailed to be convincing. The empirical results on Montezuma's Revenge show promise but lack a clear explanation of why the proposed method specifically overcomes the identified local maxima of the RND baseline.

Overview issues:

- Lack of rigorous treatment for unbounded rewards in Theorem 4: Theorem 4 claims an O*(sqrt(T)) regret bound for Gaussian MAB without bounded reward assumptions, but the proof sketch relies on truncating the rewards. The paper does not provide a formal derivation of the tail probability bounds for Gaussian distributions that would justify the truncation level Delta(eta) as a function of T. Without showing how the truncation error scales relative to the regret bound, the claim that the algorithm remains O*(sqrt(T)) is not fully supported. I suggest adding a formal lemma that bounds the expected regret contribution from the tails of the Gaussian distribution.

- Ambiguity in the definition and existence of Gaussian MAB instances: In Section 3.1, Theorem 1 and Theorem 3 establish linear lower bounds for Gaussian MAB. However, the construction of the instances relies on Assumption 1, which assumes a specific two-stage sampling process for arms. It is unclear how this assumption maps to a standard Gaussian MAB setting where rewards are drawn from a fixed joint distribution at each time step. The paper should clarify if these instances are realizable under the standard definition of Gaussian MAB or if they represent a restricted class of problems.

- Inconsistent comparison with RND baseline: The empirical study in Section 5 compares the proposed EXP4-RL algorithm against RND, but the description of the RND baseline implementation is incomplete. Specifically, the paper mentions using DQN-based RND but does not detail how the hyper-parameters were tuned for the baseline compared to the proposed algorithm. If the baseline is not tuned to its optimal performance, the claimed performance gains might be artifacts of the experimental setup. Please provide a table of hyper-parameters used for both the proposed method and the RND baseline to ensure a fair comparison.

- Computational complexity of the EXP4-RL weight update: The paper introduces an EXP4-based RL algorithm but ignores the computational overhead of maintaining expert weights in high-dimensional state spaces. In Algorithm 2, the weight update depends on the reward estimates derived from the experience buffer. If the number of experts or the size of the buffer is large, this update step could become a bottleneck. The paper should discuss the computational scalability of the weight update mechanism in the context of deep RL, where training time is already significant.

- Missing theoretical connection between EXP4-RL and regret bounds: The paper provides regret bounds for the Gaussian MAB setting and separately proposes an EXP4-based RL algorithm, but it fails to bridge these two contributions. The theoretical analysis focuses on the MAB setting, while the RL section relies entirely on empirical results without any corresponding regret analysis for the EXP4-RL agent. To deliver on the promise of an 'EXP-based algorithm for RL', the paper should provide at least a simplified regret decomposition for the RL case, perhaps by assuming a tabular MDP structure or a linear function approximation, to show how the EXP4 regret bounds translate to the RL setting.

- Absence of a concrete Gaussian MAB instance calculation: While Theorem 1 and Theorem 3 provide lower bounds for Gaussian MAB, the paper lacks a fully specified, numerical example of these bounds for a concrete parameter set. Readers would benefit from a worked calculation of the regret lower bound for a specific 2-arm Gaussian bandit with given means and covariance matrix. Computing the exact value of the regret lower bound for a specific time horizon T would clarify the role of the constants and the 'large enough T' condition mentioned in the theorems.

- Lack of sensitivity analysis for the EXP4-RL expert selection: The EXP4-RL algorithm relies on the combination of multiple experts, but the performance is presented only for a fixed two-expert configuration. The paper claims this approach enables global exploration, yet it does not demonstrate how the algorithm behaves as the number or the diversity of experts changes. Adding a sensitivity analysis or a small-scale simulation comparing the performance with varying numbers of experts (e.g., 2, 5, and 10) would be necessary to substantiate the claim that the EXP4 framework effectively manages multiple exploration strategies.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a detailed proof for the truncation argument used in Theorem 4 to justify the O*(sqrt(T)) bound for unbounded rewards.

- Clarify the relationship between Assumption 1 and the standard definition of Gaussian MAB to ensure the lower bounds are meaningful.

- Include a comprehensive hyper-parameter table for both the proposed EXP4-RL and the RND baseline to demonstrate that the performance gains are not due to differential tuning.

- Discuss the computational complexity of the weight update step and provide runtime comparisons against standard RL baselines.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in Gaussian integral notation
comments.quote: G(q,\mu)=\max\left\{\int\left|qe^{-\frac{\epsilon^{2}}{2}}-(1-q)e^{-\frac{(s-\mu)^{2}}{2}}\right|dx,\int\left|(1-q)e^{-\frac{\epsilon^{2}}{2}}-qe^{-\frac{(s-\mu)^{2}}{2}}\right|dx\right\}.
comments.explanation: The use of 'dx' as a differential for an integrand defined in terms of 's' is a notation error. Furthermore, the expression lacks the standard Gaussian normalization factor (1/sqrt(2*pi)). Without this factor, the integral does not represent a total variation distance or a probability difference, which is necessary for the inequality G(q, mu) < q to be meaningful. Please rewrite the expression using 'ds' and include the normalization factor 1/sqrt(2*pi) for both Gaussian terms.

comments.title: Inconsistent summation index in EXP4-RL probability definition
comments.explanation: The definition of rho_k uses the summation index K, which is defined in the algorithm as the number of actions. However, the weights w_k are indexed by k in {1, ..., E}, where E is the number of experts. The normalization factor must be the sum over all experts, sum_{j=1}^E w_j, to ensure sum_{k=1}^E rho_k = 1. Please correct the denominator to sum over the expert index.

comments.title: Vanishing confidence in Theorem 4
comments.quote: R_{T}\leq 4\Delta(\eta)\cdot(\sqrt{KT\log(\frac{KT}{\delta})}+4\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}KT\log K}+8\log(\frac{KT}{\delta}))\text{ with probability }(1-\delta)\cdot(1-\eta)^{T}
comments.explanation: The probability bound (1-delta)(1-eta)^T is problematic for large T. If eta > 0 is fixed, the confidence level approaches 0 as T tends to infinity, rendering the regret bound vacuous. Standard MAB literature typically uses a union bound over T steps, resulting in a failure probability of T*eta + delta. If you intend to maintain a constant confidence level, you must set eta = delta'/T. Please revise the theorem statement to reflect a non-vanishing confidence level.

comments.title: Ambiguity in RND baseline performance
comments.quote: The benchmark on Montezuma's Revenge is RND based on DQN which achieves a reward of zero in our environment (the PPO algorithm reported in Burda et al. (2018) has reward 8,000 with many more computing resources; we ran the PPO-based RND with 10 parallel environments and 800 epochs to observe that the reward is also 0)
comments.explanation: The claim that the PPO-based RND baseline achieves a reward of 0 contradicts established performance benchmarks. Burda et al. (2018) demonstrate that RND achieves significant scores on this task, and a reward of 0 suggests a potential implementation error or an issue with the environment setup rather than a limitation of the baseline. If the baseline fails to learn entirely, it is difficult to conclude that your proposed algorithm is improving upon it in a meaningful way. Please verify the implementation of the baseline and explain why it fails to match the performance reported in the literature.

comments.title: Inconsistency in reward boundedness claim
comments.quote: The analyses do not require bounded rewards compared to classical regret assumptions.
comments.explanation: This claim is misleading because your methodology in Section 3.2 relies on truncating rewards to handle the unbounded nature of Gaussian distributions. Truncation effectively imposes a bounded reward assumption on the algorithm's internal processing. It would be more accurate to state that the analysis handles unbounded rewards by explicitly controlling the truncation error, rather than claiming the analysis does not require bounded rewards.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of reward expectation in weight update
comments.quote:
Update the trust coefficient $w_{k}$ of each $Q_{k}$-network as follows:
$P_{k}=\epsilon\text{-greedy}(Q_{k}),\hat{x}_{kj}=\frac{\mathds{1}_{j=a}}{P_{kj}}(n_{r}-r_{i}),j\in 1,2,\ldots,K,y_{k}=E[\hat{x}_{kj}],w_{k}=w_{k}\cdot e^{\frac{\eta}{E}y_{k}}$
comments.explanation: The term y_k = E[x_kj] is notationally ambiguous as it does not specify the distribution over which the expectation is taken. Furthermore, the use of (n_r - r_i) implies the algorithm is minimizing a loss, but the exponential update e^{\frac{\eta}{E}y_k} increases weights for higher values of y_k. If y_k represents a loss, the exponent should be negative. Please clarify if y_k is intended to be a reward or loss estimate and adjust the sign of the exponent accordingly.

comments.title: Inconsistent pseudo regret summation notation
comments.quote: We use $R_{T}^{\prime}=T\cdot\max_{k}\mu_{k}-\sum_{t}E[y_{t}]$ to denote pseudo regret called simply regret.
comments.explanation: The summation index for the pseudo regret R_T' is underspecified, whereas the definition for R_T explicitly ranges from t=1 to T. To ensure mathematical precision and consistency, please rewrite the summation as sum_{t=1}^{T}E[y_{t}].

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes EXP4-RL, an exploration method combining EXP-family weight updates with deep Q-networks, and provides regret bounds for a Gaussian multi-armed bandit setting. While the attempt to extend exponential weighting to RL is interesting, the theoretical analysis does not carry over to the proposed algorithm, the pseudocode contains critical mathematical errors, and the experimental evaluation lacks standard baselines and statistical rigor.

The paper tackles a relevant problem by attempting to adapt EXP-family exploration to reinforcement learning and provides some theoretical analysis for unbounded Gaussian rewards. The core idea of using multiple expert policies with adaptive trust coefficients has potential, and the focus on hard-to-explore environments is appropriate. However, the disconnect between the MAB theory and the RL method, combined with implementation flaws and weak empirical validation, prevents the work from meeting publication standards.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical Disconnect Between MAB Analysis and RL Extension: Section 3 derives regret bounds for EXP3.P in a Gaussian multi-armed bandit setting, while Section 4 introduces EXP4-RL for reinforcement learning without extending the theoretical framework. The MAB analysis assumes independent, identically distributed rewards per arm, whereas RL involves Markovian state transitions, non-stationary value estimates, and function approximation. The paper offers no regret analysis for the proposed EXP4-RL algorithm, leaving a critical gap between the theoretical claims and the practical method. Readers will naturally question how bounds derived for static arm selection translate to policy optimization in an MDP. The authors should either provide a regret analysis for EXP4-RL under standard RL assumptions or clearly frame the MAB results as a preliminary step rather than a foundation for the RL algorithm.

- Mathematical Incoherence in Algorithm 2 Pseudocode: The pseudocode for EXP4-RL in Section 4 contains fundamental errors that contradict standard EXP-family weight updates. The trust coefficient update $w_k = w_k \cdot e^{\frac{\pi_k}{\epsilon}}$ uses $\pi_k$, which is defined as a policy distribution over actions, not a scalar reward estimate. Standard EXP3 and EXP4 updates multiply weights by exponentiated importance-weighted reward estimates. The algorithm also defines $P_k = \epsilon\text{-greedy}(Q_k)$ as an assignment rather than a probability distribution, and the reward estimation term $\hat{x}_{kj}$ lacks proper importance weighting for the sampled expert. These notational and mathematical inconsistencies make the algorithm impossible to implement as written. The authors must rewrite the weight update mechanism to align with established EXP-family formulations and clearly define how reward estimates are computed and fed into the trust coefficient updates.

- Triviality and Limited Scope of the Linear Regret Lower Bound: Theorem 1 and Theorem 3 establish a linear regret lower bound of $\Omega(T)$ for Gaussian MAB, but the authors explicitly restrict this result to small horizons where $T \leq \frac{\epsilon - G(q,\mu)}{(1-q)\int|\dots|} + 2$. Table 1 demonstrates that this upper bound on $T$ drops rapidly as the mean gap $\mu$ increases, rendering the linear regret regime practically irrelevant for asymptotic analysis. Claiming a 'very first regret lower bound' for unbounded Gaussian MAB overlooks that linear regret is the expected behavior when an algorithm fails to distinguish arms within the given horizon. The paper acknowledges that the $O(\sqrt{T})$ upper bound only holds for large $T$, but fails to bridge this gap or explain why the small-$T$ lower bound matters for algorithm design. A more meaningful contribution would analyze the transition horizon or provide instance-dependent bounds that reflect practical learning dynamics.

- Insufficient Baselines and Evaluation Rigor: The computational study in Section 5 compares EXP4-RL exclusively against RND, ignoring established exploration baselines such as UCBVI, count-based methods, or Thompson Sampling variants. The evaluation relies heavily on intrinsic reward curves and area-under-curve differences across manipulated burn-in periods, which introduces selection bias. No statistical significance testing across multiple random seeds is provided, and the reported AUC improvements lack confidence intervals or variance reporting. Furthermore, the experiments do not measure actual cumulative regret or sample efficiency, which are standard metrics for evaluating exploration algorithms. The authors should include a broader set of baselines, report results across multiple seeds with error bands, and use regret or success rate metrics to substantiate the exploration claims.

- Unaddressed Assumptions Regarding Function Approximation and Non-Stationarity: The theoretical analysis in Section 3 assumes Gaussian rewards with a fixed covariance matrix and independent observations, but the RL implementation in Section 4 uses deep Q-networks trained on experience replay with non-stationary targets. The paper does not discuss how the EXP4 weight updates interact with bootstrapped value estimation, distributional shift, or the curse of dimensionality in continuous state spaces. The claim that EXP4-RL enables 'global exploration' lacks theoretical or empirical justification beyond intrinsic reward spikes, which can easily result from exploration noise rather than systematic coverage. The authors need to explicitly address how the algorithm handles function approximation errors and non-stationary reward distributions, or restrict the theoretical claims to tabular MDPs where the MAB analysis might loosely apply.

- Numerical demonstration of the regret scaling transition: The paper establishes an Omega(T) lower bound for small horizons and an O(sqrt(T)) upper bound for large horizons, but provides no simulation to show this transition. Readers need to see how EXP3.P behaves on a concrete Gaussian instance to verify that the regret curve actually bends from linear to square-root. A simple simulation with a two-arm Gaussian bandit, tracking cumulative regret over T=1 to 10^5, would ground the theoretical claims. Without this demonstration, the bounds remain purely algebraic and disconnected from practical learning dynamics.

- Worked parametric example for the derived bounds: The theoretical results depend on the covariance matrix Sigma, the mean vector mu, and the truncation threshold Delta(eta), yet the paper never computes these quantities for a fully specified model. Computing the explicit bound constants for a standard two-arm Gaussian bandit with known variances and a fixed correlation coefficient would let readers calibrate the intuition behind the O*(sqrt(T)) factor. This calculation would also clarify how the covariance structure inflates or deflates the regret bound compared to the independent case. Providing this concrete example would make the abstract inequalities tangible.

- Clarification of importance weighting in EXP4-RL updates: Algorithm 2 outlines the EXP4-RL framework but leaves the computation of the reward estimates x_hat_kj ambiguous. The text defines P_k as the epsilon-greedy policy of Q_k, but it does not explain how to weight the observed reward when the expert k_bar is selected with probability rho_k_bar rather than P_k_bar(a). Standard EXP-family algorithms require precise importance weighting to correct for the sampling distribution mismatch. The authors must specify the exact formula for x_hat_kj and explain how the intrinsic reward is incorporated into the trust coefficient update to make the method reproducible.

- Analysis of the transition horizon between regret regimes: The paper acknowledges that the linear lower bound holds for T bounded by a specific function of mu and epsilon, and the square-root upper bound requires large T, but it does not discuss the gap between these regimes. Readers will wonder how the transition horizon scales with the mean gap mu and the covariance structure. A brief discussion or plot showing how the transition point shifts as mu changes would clarify the practical relevance of the linear regret result. This would bridge the gap between the theoretical bounds and the algorithm's actual performance window.

Recommendation: Major revision. The theoretical analysis does not extend to the proposed RL algorithm, the pseudocode contains critical mathematical errors, and the experimental evaluation lacks standard baselines and statistical rigor. These issues fundamentally undermine the paper's claims and reproducibility, requiring substantial theoretical and empirical work before it can be considered for publication.

Revision targets:

- Correct the weight update mechanism in Algorithm 2 to match standard EXP-family importance-weighted reward estimates and fix all notational inconsistencies in the pseudocode.

- Provide a regret analysis for EXP4-RL in tabular MDPs or clearly separate the MAB theory from the RL method to avoid false theoretical implications.

- Replace the small-horizon $\Omega(T)$ lower bound with an instance-dependent or asymptotic analysis that bridges the gap to the $O(\sqrt{T})$ upper bound.

- Expand the experimental evaluation to include standard exploration baselines (UCBVI, count-based, Thompson Sampling), report results across multiple seeds with confidence intervals, and measure cumulative regret or success rates.

comments.title: Divergent Integrals in Lower Bound Definitions
comments.quote: G(q,\mu)=\max\left\{\int\left|qe^{-\frac{\epsilon^{2}}{2}}-(1-q)e^{-\frac{(s-\mu)^{2}}{2}}\right|dx,\int\left|(1-q)e^{-\frac{\epsilon^{2}}{2}}-qe^{-\frac{(s-\mu)^{2}}{2}}\right|dx\right\}.
comments.explanation: The integrals as written integrate a constant term $e^{-\epsilon^2/2}$ over the entire real line, which mathematically diverges. The notation clearly intends to compute the $L_1$ distance between the mixture densities of the inferior and superior arms. The constant $\epsilon$ should be the integration variable $s$, and the differential should match. Furthermore, Gaussian PDFs require the $1/\sqrt{2\pi}$ normalization factor. Rewrite the integrals using $e^{-s^2/2}$ instead of $e^{-\epsilon^2/2}$, add the normalization factor, and use $ds$ as the differential to ensure convergence and correct probabilistic interpretation.

comments.title: Horizon Bound Formula Contradicts Table 1
comments.quote: T\leq\frac{\epsilon-G(q,\mu)}{(1-q)\cdot\int\left|e^{-\frac{\epsilon^{2}}{2}}-e^{-\frac{(s-\mu)^{2}}{2}}\right|}+2
comments.explanation: This formula for the horizon $T$ contains the same typographical error as the definition of $G(q,\mu)$. The term $e^{-\epsilon^2/2}$ is constant with respect to the integration variable, causing the denominator to diverge. As written, the denominator is infinite, implying $T \leq 2$, which directly contradicts Table 1 where $T$ reaches values like 25001. Replace $e^{-\epsilon^2/2}$ with $e^{-s^2/2}$ in the denominator's integrand to eliminate the divergence and restore mathematical consistency with the table.

comments.title: Vanishing Success Probability in Theorem 4
comments.quote: \text{ with probability }(1-\delta)\cdot(1-\eta)^{T}
comments.explanation: For any fixed $\eta \in (0,1)$, the factor $(1-\eta)^T$ decays exponentially to zero as $T \to \infty$. This means the stated high-probability guarantee actually fails with probability approaching 1 for large horizons, which defeats the purpose of a high-probability bound. In standard analyses, the failure probability parameter must scale with $T$ (e.g., $\eta = 1/T$) to keep the success probability bounded away from zero. Specify $\eta$ as a function of $T$ to prevent the success probability from vanishing.

comments.title: Misapplication of Jensen's Inequality
comments.quote: xt. It is easy to verify by the Jensen’s inequality that $R_{T}^{\prime}\leq E[R_{T}]$
comments.explanation: Jensen's inequality is misapplied here. In any MAB setting with unbiased reward observations, linearity of expectation directly yields $E[R_T] = R_T'$, making the inequality an equality rather than a strict bound derived from convexity. Jensen's inequality relates $\mathbb{E}[g(X)]$ to $g(\mathbb{E}[X])$ for convex $g$, which has no bearing on comparing pseudo-regret to expected actual regret. Remove the reference to Jensen’s inequality and rely on linearity of expectation.

comments.title: Insufficient Justification for Unbounded Tail Truncation
comments.quote: Since a Gaussian distribution is a light-tailed distribution we can control the probability of tail shrinking which leads to the overall result.
comments.explanation: Bounding only the probability of tail events is insufficient for unbounded rewards. Regret depends on the magnitude of rewards in the tail, not just their frequency. For Gaussian rewards, tail events can produce arbitrarily large regret values, so a valid truncation argument must bound the expected loss from the tail. Add a rigorous bound on the expected tail contribution to regret, specifically $\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t=1}^T (r_{i^*}(t) - r_{i_t}(t)) \mathbb{1}_{\text{tail}}]$, to close this gap.

comments.title: Vague Proof Sketch for Theorem 6
comments.quote: k. We show an upper bound on $E[R_{T}]$ by using select inequalities, limit theories, and Randemacher complexity. To t
comments.explanation: This statement provides no verifiable mathematical detail. Phrases like "select inequalities, limit theories, and Randemacher complexity" are not specific tools or steps that can be evaluated. The proof sketch must explicitly state which inequalities (e.g., Hoeffding, Bernstein), which limit theorems, and how Rademacher complexity is applied. Replace the vague phrase with explicit mathematical steps to make the claim verifiable.

comments.title: Numerical Values Contradict Comparative Claim
comments.quote: behavior. EXP4-RL improves on RND as training progresses, e.g. the improvement after 550 epochs is higher than the one between epochs 250 and 550. In terms for AUC, this is expressed by  $1.6\%$  and  $3.5\%$ , respectively.
comments.explanation: The paragraph claims that the performance gain after 550 epochs is higher than the gain observed between epochs 250 and 550. The subsequent sentence reports these gains as 1.6% and 3.5%, respectively. Because 1.6% is strictly less than 3.5%, the numerical values directly contradict the comparative statement. Recompute the area-under-curve differences for these specific time intervals and update the text so the numbers and the claim align.

comments.title: Inappropriate Evaluation Metric for EXP-Based Algorithm
comments.quote: We use the same criterion for evaluating exploration performance of our algorithm and RND herein.
comments.explanation: Using RND's prediction-error metric to evaluate an EXP-based algorithm is methodologically inconsistent. The proposed method relies on exponential weighting of expert trust coefficients, not on state prediction error. Measuring EXP4-RL's exploration with a metric designed for RND conflates the evaluation criterion with the algorithm's actual exploration mechanism. Replace this sentence with a standard exploration metric or add a paragraph justifying why RND's intrinsic reward is theoretically appropriate for measuring EXP-family exploration.

comments.title: Contradictory Baseline Description
comments.quote:
We use RND on DQN from Rivlin (2019) as the baseline and show the real reward improvement of Algorithm 2, which supports the intuition and superiority of the algorithm.

The comparison between Algorithm 2 and RND is presented in Figure 1.
comments.explanation: The paragraph explicitly identifies the baseline as RND on DQN, but the final sentence compares the proposed method to the RND counterpart without the DQN expert. This contradiction leaves readers unsure whether the DQN expert is part of the baseline or the proposed algorithm. Clarify the exact configuration of the baseline curve and ensure the textual description aligns with the visual data.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Linear Lower Bound Validity
comments.quote: . In our case the upper bound result $O^{*}(\sqrt{T})$ holds for large enough $T$ which is hidden behind $O^{*}$ while the linear lower bounds is valid only for small values
comments.explanation: This claim fundamentally misunderstands the nature of regret lower bounds. Lower bounds characterize worst-case behavior across the entire horizon, not just small values of $T$. Asserting that a linear lower bound is "valid only for small values of $T$" contradicts the definition of a lower bound. If the authors mean that the linear lower bound is loose for large $T$ compared to the $\sqrt{T}$ upper bound, they should clarify that the lower bound is tight only in specific regimes, rather than claiming it is invalid for large $T$.

comments.title: Unjustified Removal of Δ Dependence in Theorem 5
comments.quote:
The dependence of the bound on $\Delta$ can be removed by considering large enough $T$ as stated next.

###
comments.explanation: The transition from Theorem 4 to Theorem 5 lacks mathematical justification. Theorem 4's bound scales linearly with $\Delta(\eta)$, which is a constant determined by the Gaussian quantile. Simply stating that the dependence "can be removed by considering large enough $T$" is mathematically incomplete. The paper must explicitly demonstrate how the $\Delta$-dependent term is absorbed or shown to be subdominant for large $T$. Add a brief derivation showing that the tail regret contribution is $o(\sqrt{T})$ as $T \to \infty$.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper attempts two distinct contributions: regret bounds for Gaussian MAB using EXP3.P, and an extension of EXP4 to multi-agent RL exploration. The theoretical regret analysis is incomplete and contains gaps, while the empirical RL results lack statistical rigor and comparison to proper baselines. The paper's main value is the conceptual idea of using EXP4 for RL exploration, but the current execution does not convincingly demonstrate either theoretical or empirical validity.

Overview issues:

- Gap between lower and upper regret bounds is not resolved: The paper claims a lower bound of Ω(T) for small T and an upper bound of O*(√T) for large T, but these bounds apply to different regimes and are not directly comparable. The lower bound (Theorem 1) holds only for T ≤ 2501 for μ=10⁻⁴, while the upper bound (Theorem 5) holds for 'large enough T' without specifying the threshold. This means there is no T for which both bounds are simultaneously valid, so the paper does not establish tight regret bounds for Gaussian MAB. The authors acknowledge this gap but do not discuss its implications for the optimality of EXP3.P in this setting. A meaningful regret analysis would require bounds that apply to the same range of T.

- Lower bound construction relies on unrealistic assumptions: Theorem 1's lower bound depends on Assumption 1, which requires the player to first choose between 'superior' and 'inferior' arm sets with known probabilities q and 1-q, and then sample an arm from the chosen set. This is not a standard bandit protocol — the player does not choose arms directly. The assumption that the player knows the proportion of superior arms (q) is also unrealistic. The resulting lower bound of Ω(T) is a consequence of this artificial construction rather than a fundamental property of Gaussian MAB. The paper should either justify why this assumption is natural or provide a lower bound under the standard bandit protocol.

- Upper bound proof relies on truncation without rigorous justification: Theorem 4's proof truncates Gaussian rewards to a bounded range [-Δ, Δ] and then applies the bounded EXP3.P bound from Auer et al. (2002b). However, the paper does not show how the truncation error propagates through the EXP3.P weight updates. The truncation level Δ(η) depends on the joint Gaussian density, but the regret bound includes a factor Δ(η) that could be arbitrarily large for small η. Theorem 5 claims to remove this dependence for large T, but the proof is not provided in the main text and the appendix is not included. Without seeing the proof, it is impossible to verify whether the O*(√T) bound is valid or whether it hides constants that depend on the Gaussian parameters in problematic ways.

- EXP4-RL algorithm is not properly connected to EXP4 theory: The paper presents EXP4-RL as an extension of EXP4 to RL, but the algorithm differs from EXP4 in several critical ways that are not theoretically justified. In EXP4, experts provide advice vectors over actions; in EXP4-RL, experts are Q-networks that are trained on the same replay buffer. The trust coefficient update in Algorithm 2 uses 'E[ˆx_kj]' which is not defined in terms of observable quantities, and the update rule w_k = w_k·exp(π_k/ε) appears to have a typo (π_k is not defined). The paper does not provide any regret guarantee for EXP4-RL, nor does it explain how the EXP4 regret bounds would transfer to the RL setting with non-stationary rewards and temporal credit assignment. Without theoretical grounding, the algorithm is essentially heuristic.

- Empirical evaluation lacks statistical significance and proper baselines: The experiments on Montezuma's Revenge compare EXP4-RL only to a DQN-based RND baseline, not to the original PPO-based RND that achieves 8,000 reward. The paper dismisses this discrepancy by saying 'we ran the PPO-based RND with 10 parallel environments and 800 epochs to observe that the reward is also 0', but this is insufficient — the original RND paper used 64 parallel environments and 1.8 billion frames. The paper does not report standard deviations, confidence intervals, or number of random seeds. The AUC improvements of 1.6% and 3.5% in Figure 3b are tiny and could easily be due to random variation. The claim of '146.0%' improvement in Figure 2c is misleading because the baseline RND has near-zero intrinsic reward in that regime.

- Missing ablation study isolating the effect of EXP4 exploration: The paper attributes the performance improvement to EXP4's ability to combine multiple exploration experts, but does not ablate this mechanism. A proper ablation would compare EXP4-RL against: (a) a simple ensemble of Q-networks without EXP4 weighting, (b) a single Q-network with the same total compute budget, and (c) random selection between RND and DQN experts. Without these controls, it is unclear whether the improvement comes from the EXP4 trust coefficient update, the ensemble of Q-networks, or simply having more parameters. The paper claims 'global exploration' but provides no evidence that the trust coefficients actually switch between experts at appropriate times.

- Contribution is overstated relative to actual results: The paper claims 'the very first regret lower bound' for Gaussian MAB and 'the very first extension of EXP4 to RL exploration'. However, the lower bound is for a non-standard bandit protocol and only holds for small T, while the upper bound is asymptotic. The EXP4-RL extension is heuristic without theoretical guarantees. The empirical results show modest improvements on a single game (Montezuma's Revenge) with a weak baseline. The paper would benefit from more measured claims that acknowledge the limitations: the lower bound is for a restricted setting, the upper bound is asymptotic, and the RL extension is an empirical heuristic that needs more validation.

- No worked example for Gaussian MAB lower bound: The lower bound construction in Theorem 1 relies on Assumption 1, which is non-standard. The paper should compute the lower bound for a concrete, fully-specified Gaussian bandit with, say, K=2, μ=0.1, and Σ=I, under the standard bandit protocol (no prior knowledge of arm types). This would show whether the Ω(T) bound is an artifact of the assumption or a genuine property of Gaussian MAB. Without such an example, readers cannot calibrate how restrictive Assumption 1 is or whether the lower bound has bite in practice.

- Missing simulation verifying EXP3.P regret bounds: The paper derives upper and lower bounds for EXP3.P in Gaussian MAB but provides no simulation to demonstrate that these bounds are realized. A standard simulation would generate rewards from a multivariate normal with known μ and Σ, run EXP3.P for various T, and plot the empirical regret against the theoretical O*(√T) and Ω(T) curves. This would verify that the bounds are not vacuous and would illustrate the regime where the lower bound applies (small T) versus where the upper bound applies (large T). The absence of such a simulation is a notable gap for a paper claiming regret bounds.

- EXP4-RL lacks theoretical justification and implementation guidance: The paper presents EXP4-RL as an extension of EXP4 to RL but provides no regret guarantee for the RL setting, nor does it discuss how the non-stationarity of rewards in an MDP affects the EXP4 trust coefficient updates. The algorithm description includes undefined quantities (e.g., 'E[ˆx_kj]' and 'π_k') and does not explain how the curse of dimensionality in state-action spaces is handled. To be publishable, the paper should either provide a theoretical analysis (even for a simplified MDP) or, at minimum, discuss why the EXP4 update remains sensible given temporal credit assignment. Additionally, practical guidance on hyperparameter selection (e.g., Δ, ε, η, z, τ) is missing, which limits reproducibility.

- No comparison to existing multi-expert exploration methods: The paper claims EXP4-RL is the first extension of EXP4 to RL exploration, but does not compare it to existing multi-expert or ensemble exploration methods in RL, such as bootstrapped DQN (Osband et al., 2016) or randomized prior functions (Osband et al., 2018). A formal comparison—either theoretical (showing EXP4-RL recovers prior methods as special cases) or empirical (comparing performance on the same benchmarks)—is needed to position the contribution. Without this, the claim of novelty is unsubstantiated.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper has interesting ideas but the theoretical analysis is incomplete and the empirical evaluation is insufficient. The lower and upper bounds do not match, the lower bound relies on unrealistic assumptions, and the RL experiments lack statistical rigor. A revision would need to either provide matching bounds for the same T regime or significantly strengthen the empirical validation with proper baselines and statistical tests.

Revision targets:

- Provide regret bounds (lower and upper) that apply to the same range of T, or clearly state the regime for each bound and explain why the gap is acceptable.

- Derive a lower bound under the standard bandit protocol (without Assumption 1) or justify why Assumption 1 is natural and how it relates to standard protocols.

- Include the full proof of Theorem 5 in the appendix and verify that the O*(√T) bound does not hide problematic dependence on Gaussian parameters.

- Add an ablation study comparing EXP4-RL against ensemble baselines without EXP4 weighting, and report results with multiple random seeds, standard deviations, and confidence intervals.

- Compare EXP4-RL against the original PPO-based RND with comparable computational resources, or justify why the DQN-based RND is the appropriate baseline.

comments.title: Lower bound construction uses non-standard bandit protocol
comments.quote:
. We need the following assumption or setting.

###### Assumption 1

There are two types of arms with general $K$ with one type being superior ($S$ is the set of superior arms) and the other being inferior ($I$ is the set of inferior arms). Let $1-q,q$ be the proportions of the superior and inferior arms, respectively which is known to the adversary and clearly $0\leq q\leq 1$. The arms in $S$ are indistinguishable and so are those in $I$. The first pull of the player has two steps. In the first step the player selects an inferior or superior set of arms based on $P(S)=1-q$ and $P(I)=q$ and once a set is selected, the corresponding reward of an arm from the selec
comments.explanation: Assumption 1 describes a setting where the player knows the proportions q and 1−q of inferior and superior arms, and the first pull involves selecting a set (not an arm) with known probabilities. This is not the standard multi-armed bandit setting, where the player chooses an arm directly and receives its reward. The assumption fundamentally changes the problem: it gives the player prior knowledge about the proportion of good arms and a special first step. The paper claims to derive lower bounds for 'Gaussian MAB' and 'general MAB', but the results only apply under this non-standard assumption. The abstract and introduction do not mention this restriction, which misrepresents the scope of the results. The authors should either rename the setting (e.g., 'structured MAB with known proportions') or justify why Assumption 1 does not reduce generality.

comments.title: Theorem 4 truncation argument lacks rigorous justification
comments.quote:
1-\eta$.

In the proof of Theorem 4, we first perform truncation of the rewards of Gaussian MAB by dividing the rewards to a bounded part and unbounded tail throughout the game. For the bounded part, we directly borrow the regret upper bound of EXP3.P in *Auer et al. (2002b)* and conclude with the regret upper bound of order
comments.explanation: The paper claims to 'directly borrow' the regret bound from Auer et al. (2002b) for the bounded part, but that bound assumes rewards are bounded in [0,1]. After truncation, the bounded part has range [-Δ, Δ] (or similar), so the bound from Auer et al. would need to be scaled by Δ. The paper does not specify the scaling factor or verify that the truncated rewards satisfy the conditions of the original EXP3.P analysis (e.g., the importance-weighted estimators may behave differently under truncation). The statement 'conclude with the regret upper bound of order O(Δ(η)√T)' is too vague. The paper should provide the explicit scaling argument and confirm that the truncated rewards still satisfy the assumptions of the original bound.

comments.title: No regret guarantee for EXP4-RL in MDP setting
comments.quote: EXP4 has shown great success in contextual bandits. Therefore, in this section, we extend EXP4 to RL and develop EXP4-RL illustrated in Algorithm 2.
comments.explanation: The paper claims to extend EXP4 to RL but provides no theoretical regret guarantee for the resulting algorithm. EXP4's regret bounds rely on the contextual bandit assumption that rewards are i.i.d. given the context and that expert advice is fixed at the start of the game. In RL, rewards depend on the agent's actions through MDP dynamics, and expert Q-networks are trained online, making advice non-stationary. The paper does not discuss how these differences affect the validity of EXP4's regret analysis. Without a theoretical guarantee, the algorithm is heuristic. The authors should either provide a regret analysis for a simplified MDP setting or explicitly state that the extension is empirical and lacks theoretical justification.

comments.title: Missing quantitative results and statistical rigor in computational study
comments.quote: As a numerical demonstration of the superior performance and exploration incentive of Algorithm 2, we show the improvements on baselines on two hard-to-explore RL games, Mountain Car and Montezuma's Revenge.
comments.explanation: The section promises a 'numerical demonstration' but provides no numbers, tables, or figures—only qualitative descriptions. The reader cannot evaluate the claimed 'superior performance' or 'remarkable improvement' without seeing actual reward values, standard deviations, or number of runs. This is a critical omission for a computational study. The authors must include quantitative results (e.g., mean and standard error of cumulative reward over multiple seeds) for both games, and compare against the baselines mentioned.

comments.title: Missing ablation study isolating the effect of EXP4 exploration
comments.quote: Overall, EXP4-RL improves RND local minima of exploration, keeps high exploration of RND and induces a smoother global exploration.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that EXP4-RL improves exploration, but does not ablate the EXP4 mechanism. A proper ablation would compare EXP4-RL against: (a) a simple ensemble of two Q-networks without EXP4 weighting, (b) a single Q-network with the same total compute budget, and (c) random selection between RND and DQN experts. Without these controls, it is unclear whether the improvement comes from the EXP4 trust coefficient update, the ensemble of Q-networks, or simply having more parameters. The authors should add at least one ablation experiment to isolate the effect of the EXP4 weighting.

comments.title: Non-integer T bound in Table 1
comments.explanation: The table reports an upper bound of T = 3.5 for μ = 0.1. Since T is the time horizon (number of rounds), it must be an integer. A bound of 3.5 is not meaningful — the actual integer bound would be floor(3.5) = 3, but the paper does not specify rounding. Moreover, the bound T ≤ 3.5 means the theorem only applies to T = 1, 2, 3, which is an extremely narrow regime. The authors should either round down to 3 or explain how non-integer T bounds should be interpreted.

comments.title: AUC improvement percentages are not statistically supported
comments.quote: The relative difference in the areas under the curves are  $6.9\%$ ,  $17.0\%$ ,  $146.0\%$ , respectively, which quantifies the much better performance of EXP4-R
comments.explanation: The paper reports AUC improvements of 6.9%, 17.0%, and 146.0% for intrinsic reward curves in Figure 2, but provides no confidence intervals, standard deviations, or number of random seeds. Without statistical testing, these values could be due to random variation, especially given the high variance typical of Montezuma's Revenge. The 146.0% improvement in Figure 2c is particularly suspect because the baseline RND intrinsic reward is near zero in that regime, making percentage improvements inflated and misleading. The authors should report mean and standard error over at least 5 random seeds, and use a proper metric (e.g., absolute difference) when baseline values are near zero.

comments.title: No evidence that trust coefficients actually switch between experts
comments.quote: EXP4-RL improves on RND as training progresses, e.g. the improvement after 550 epochs is higher than the one between epochs 250 and 550.
comments.explanation: The paper attributes EXP4-RL's performance to its ability to combine multiple exploration experts via trust coefficients, but never presents any analysis of the trust coefficient dynamics. Without showing how the weights w_k evolve over time, the claim that EXP4-RL 'switches' between experts or 'improves local minima' is unsupported. The observed improvement could simply come from having an ensemble of Q-networks (more parameters) rather than from the EXP4 weighting mechanism. The authors should include a plot of trust coefficients over training epochs to demonstrate that the algorithm actually selects different experts at different times, and compare against a simple average of the two Q-networks.

comments.title: PPO-based RND baseline claim is inconsistent with original results
comments.quote: ur environment (the PPO algorithm reported in Burda et al. (2018) has reward 8,000 with many more computing resources; we ran the PPO-based RND with 10 parallel environments and 800 epochs to observe that the reward is also 0), whi
comments.explanation: The authors claim that running PPO-based RND with 10 parallel environments and 800 epochs yields zero reward, but this does not replicate the original RND setup. Burda et al. (2018) used 64 parallel environments and 1.8 billion frames (approximately 1.8e9 / 4 = 450 million steps, far exceeding 800 epochs). The paper does not specify epoch length or total frames, so the comparison is not meaningful. The statement 'the reward is also 0' is misleading because it implies the original result is not reproducible, when in fact the authors used far fewer resources. Rewrite as: 'We ran PPO-based RND with 10 parallel environments for 800 epochs (approximately X frames) and observed zero reward, which is consistent with the need for many more parallel environments and training steps as reported in Burda et al. (2018).'

comments.title: n_r initialization as -∞ is impractical
comments.quote: $n_{r}=-\infty$ (an upper bound on reward)
comments.explanation: Initializing n_r to -∞ is mathematically convenient but computationally impractical. In practice, one would initialize n_r to a very small number (e.g., -1e9) or to the first observed reward. The algorithm should specify a practical initialization, such as setting n_r to the first reward encountered or to a sufficiently small constant.
